title: One In 8,000

jokes.text: It's a long story, but the short version is, I'm currently catfishing Anthony Kiedis.
jokes.setup: April explains how she got Red Hot Chili Peppers to donate a signed guitar
jokes.punchline: The casual delivery of an elaborate deception involving a rock star
jokes.explanation: Perfect April character comedy - the absurd premise delivered with complete deadpan seriousness. High marks for character integration and originality.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I told them I was dying and only had six months to live. They sent me a signed guitar, a handwritten note, and front row tickets to their next show.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm not dying. I just really wanted that guitar.

jokes.text: if I'm gonna perform Islands In the Stream with a Sacagawea hologram
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining what the charity auction needs to fund
jokes.punchline: The completely over-the-top concept of duetting with a hologram of a historical figure
jokes.explanation: Classic Leslie overambition taken to absurd extremes. Good character integration but the structure is more throwaway than setup/payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We need to raise money for the Parks Department, and I have the perfect idea. We'll have a charity auction where I duet with a hologram of a historical figure.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, that's... that's not a real thing we can do.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Not with that attitude! I'm thinking me and Eleanor Roosevelt, doing a medley of showtunes. Or maybe Abraham Lincoln doing a rap battle.

jokes.text: No, but my brain is about to explode from stress.
jokes.setup: Leslie asks if Andy has told anyone about the baby
jokes.punchline: Andy's dramatic overreaction to keeping a simple secret
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Andy's inability to handle secrets, but fairly standard execution.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Have you told anyone about the baby?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No! I haven't told a soul. I've been keeping it locked up tight. I haven't even told Ben, I haven't told my mom, I haven't told the squirrels outside my window who I talk to every morning.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: This is the hardest thing I've ever done. Harder than learning to read. Harder than the time I ate a whole wheel of cheese.

jokes.text: That's exactly what I told Kyle when he told me his wife was cheating on him.
jokes.setup: Andy claims he's not good at keeping secrets
jokes.punchline: He immediately proves it by revealing someone else's secret as an example
jokes.explanation: Good structural joke that demonstrates Andy's point through his own actions. Well-integrated to character.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm not good at keeping secrets.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Like, Ben told me he's actually bald and wears a toupee, and I just blurted it out!

jokes.text: He's in Witness Protection.
jokes.setup: Andy mentions his neighbor Eric has kept a secret for 20 years
jokes.punchline: The casually revealed extreme nature of the secret
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and economy, though the witness protection trope is somewhat familiar territory.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: My neighbor Eric kept a secret for 20 years.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Turns out he's been a serial killer the whole time.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I was like, 'Eric, dude, you could've just told me you didn't like me!'

jokes.text: [Shouts] What? Ron Swanson asking for help?
jokes.setup: Ron says he needs to ask for a favor
jokes.punchline: Donna's shocked reaction to Ron's uncharacteristic request
jokes.explanation: Good character moment that plays off Ron's established independence, though the execution is straightforward.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I need to ask you for a favor.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Ron Swanson asking for a favor? That's like seeing a unicorn made of wood.

jokes.text: Keep your voice down, woman!
jokes.setup: Donna's loud reaction to Ron asking for help
jokes.punchline: Ron's embarrassed attempt to maintain his tough image
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment but fairly predictable Ron behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Donna, I need your help with something.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: WHAT?! Ron Swanson needs HELP?!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Please keep your voice down. And yes, I... require assistance.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: This is the best day of my life!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: If you tell anyone about this, I will have you reassigned to the parks department in the Ramsett Park area.

jokes.text: He's my Tammy.
jokes.setup: Donna explains her problematic relationship with Joe
jokes.punchline: Using Ron's famous nemesis as the comparison point
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and character integration. Uses established show mythology effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Joe and I have a complicated relationship. It's like Ron and the government.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: We're locked in an eternal struggle, neither of us will ever fully win, and we both secretly enjoy the conflict.

jokes.text: Tammy two once seduced a coroner and had me declared legally dead just so she could get a discount on a plane ticket.
jokes.setup: Ron explains that his ex-wives are the worst people in the world
jokes.punchline: The completely over-the-top lengths Tammy went to for a minor savings
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that takes Tammy's established manipulative nature to absurd extremes. Highly quotable and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Tammy once drove forty miles out of her way to a different grocery store because they had a five-cent coupon on eggs.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Five cents. She spent three dollars in gas to save a nickel.

jokes.text: I go insane.
jokes.setup: Donna explains how Joe affects her
jokes.punchline: The simple, matter-of-fact delivery of losing her mind
jokes.explanation: Good economy and deadpan delivery, though the concept is relatively simple.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Joe makes me lose my mind. I can't think straight around him. My words get all jumbled up. I start sweating. My heart races. It's like my brain just stops working.

jokes.text: I understand this problem well and agree to this exchange of services.
jokes.setup: Ron asks Donna to keep Joe away from him
jokes.punchline: Donna's overly formal business-like response to a personal favor
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Donna's occasional formal side, though not groundbreaking.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Donna, I need you to keep Joe away from me.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I'll prepare a formal memo and file it in triplicate.

jokes.text: Someone say 'Saperstein'? Come on, you two. Let's saddle you up and put some jelly on the belly.
jokes.setup: Leslie mentions asking Dr. Saperstein
jokes.punchline: Dr. Saperstein's immediate appearance with bizarre medical euphemisms
jokes.explanation: Good character introduction with memorable phrasing, though the structure is simple.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: I should ask Dr. Saperstein about this.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Saperstein
dialogue.line: Did someone say Saperstein? I'm here to help with all your medical needs, from your love apples to your tummy banana.

jokes.text: He's a little weird.
jokes.setup: Dr. Saperstein's bizarre entrance and language
jokes.punchline: Leslie's massive understatement about his obvious weirdness
jokes.explanation: Good understatement but fairly standard execution of the trope.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So Ben's dad is a little... unconventional.

jokes.text: Oh, I forgot, other hand. Crap.
jokes.setup: Andy checks his schedule written on his hands
jokes.punchline: He forgot he had information on both hands
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy that fits Andy's character perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh, I forgot I had information on both hands.

jokes.text: You get twice the secrets.
jokes.setup: April explains that married couples should share secrets
jokes.punchline: Her transactional view of marriage secrets
jokes.explanation: Perfect April logic delivered with her characteristic deadpan style.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Married couples should share secrets. It's like a bank account. You deposit secrets, you withdraw secrets, you get interest on your secrets.

jokes.text: You're acting weird. Excuse me. I have to get back to work now.
jokes.setup: April says Andy is acting weird about the secret
jokes.punchline: Andy's defensive deflection while clearly acting weird
jokes.explanation: Standard defensive behavior comedy, though it fits Andy's character.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You're acting weird about the secret.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm not acting weird! Why would you say that? I'm totally normal. Everything is fine. Nothing is wrong.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: See, you're doing it right now.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Doing what? I don't know what you're talking about.

jokes.text: I'm getting a picture of you volunteering at a public elementary school in case I ever need to blackmail you.
jokes.setup: Donna takes a photo of Ron at the school
jokes.punchline: Her openly stated intention to use it for blackmail
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment that combines Donna's scheming nature with the absurdity of blackmailing someone for doing good.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I'm taking this photo of Ron for blackmail purposes.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: What? Donna, you can't just—

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I already did. And it's going in my files.

jokes.text: My name is Ron Swanson. I am Donna's work-proximity associate.
jokes.setup: Ron introduces himself to Joe
jokes.punchline: His formal distancing from the concept of friendship
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson moment - the formal language to avoid admitting to friendship is quintessentially him.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm Ron Swanson. I don't believe in friendship. I like people just enough to not have to commit murder.

jokes.text: I'm onto you, friend. Tread lightly.
jokes.setup: Joe is being perfectly nice and helpful
jokes.punchline: Ron's inexplicable hostility toward genuine kindness
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Ron's suspicion of niceness, though the setup is straightforward.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't trust him. Something's not right.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, he's just being nice.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Exactly. Nobody is that nice. He's up to something.

jokes.text: The baby has two bodies?
jokes.setup: Doctor reveals there's another baby on the ultrasound
jokes.punchline: Ben's confused interpretation of twins
jokes.explanation: Good confused dad moment with nice economy in the phrasing.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Wait, there's two of them? How is that possible?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Congratulations, you're having twins.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: So Leslie's been cheating on me?

jokes.text: And here's a fourth. I'm so sorry, no. It's a little fleck of cream cheese on the screen.
jokes.setup: Doctor appears to find a fourth baby
jokes.punchline: It's just cream cheese on the screen
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that builds tension then releases it with an absurd explanation. Well-structured joke.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Saperstein
dialogue.line: It's just cream cheese on the screen

jokes.text: Wow, you guys really dodged a bullet. Can you imagine raising four children at one time? Ha, a nightmare.
jokes.setup: Ben and Leslie just learned they're having triplets
jokes.punchline: Doctor acts like three kids is no big deal compared to four
jokes.explanation: Good ironic understatement that shows the doctor's obliviousness to their actual situation.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Saperstein
dialogue.line: Triplets? That's wonderful news. I had quadruplets with my third wife. Well, technically my fourth wife had them, but I was there for most of it.

jokes.text: If we do this right, we can be parents to 1/3 of the supreme court!
jokes.setup: Leslie and Ben just learned they're having triplets
jokes.punchline: Leslie's immediate political ambitions for her unborn children
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - turning a personal moment into political aspirations. Excellent character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We're having triplets! That's three future city council members, three future state senators, and one future president. I'm thinking two of them run for president in 2052, they split the vote, and the third one becomes Supreme Court Justice and settles it.

jokes.text: It's what we doctors like to call-- and I don't mean to be insensitive-- a going-out-of-business sale.
jokes.setup: Doctor explains why older women release multiple eggs
jokes.punchline: Comparing reproductive biology to a retail clearance
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy that combines medical explanation with inappropriate retail metaphor. Highly quotable and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Saperstein
dialogue.line: As women age, their ovaries release multiple eggs as a sort of biological clearance event. It's like when a store realizes it's stuck with merchandise nobody wants, so they just put everything on sale at once.

jokes.text: how-- what do we do?
jokes.setup: Ben trying to ask what to do about triplets
jokes.punchline: His complete verbal breakdown and stuttering
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Ben's overwhelmed state, though the execution is fairly standard.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I... I don't... we're having... Leslie's pregnant. With triplets. I can't... what do I... how do you even... there's three of them. Three babies. At the same time. I'm not... I've never... oh God.

jokes.text: Leslie, I need you to be Buddha on quaaludes, and, Ben, you are Matthew McConaughey in a hammock on the beach.
jokes.setup: Doctor prescribes rest and avoiding stress
jokes.punchline: His bizarre celebrity/drug metaphors for relaxation
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist humor with perfect specificity. The unexpected metaphors are both funny and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Saperstein
dialogue.line: You need complete rest and to avoid all stress.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Saperstein
dialogue.line: Think of stress like a celebrity—you need to avoid it like it's Lindsay Lohan in 2007.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Saperstein
dialogue.line: And relaxation? Relaxation is like heroin. You want more and more of it, and eventually it'll kill you.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Saperstein
dialogue.line: Actually, scratch that—relaxation is like a non-lethal heroin. A completely safe heroin with no side effects that also cures cancer.

jokes.text: All right, all right. All right.
jokes.setup: Doctor mentions Matthew McConaughey
jokes.punchline: Ben immediately does McConaughey's famous catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Good immediate callback with nice economy, though it's somewhat predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Matthew McConaughey

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Alright, alright, alright.

jokes.text: I just multiplied all our future expenses by three, and you know what happened? The numbers got a lot higher.
jokes.setup: Ben is panicking about the cost of raising triplets
jokes.punchline: His explanation of basic multiplication as if it's surprising
jokes.explanation: Great deadpan delivery of obvious mathematical facts. Perfect for Ben's accountant character.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Do you know what three times anything is? It's three of that thing! Three times the diapers, three times the formula, three times the tiny clothes that they grow out of in like five minutes!

jokes.text: I think I heard the computer laugh at me.
jokes.setup: Ben is calculating their financial situation
jokes.punchline: His paranoid interpretation of computer sounds
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist touch that shows Ben's stressed state through paranoid thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Okay, so if we liquidate our savings, sell the house, and I start a underground poker game in the basement, we could be solvent by... oh no.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Did you hear that? The computer just made that beeping sound. That's the IRS. They're in the walls.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: And that whirring noise? That's them accessing my browser history. I knew I shouldn't have Googled 'how to hide money legally.'

jokes.text: Oh, I will be there in two flaps of a butterfly's wing, and I love you.
jokes.setup: Ben asks if Leslie is ready to go, trying to keep her calm
jokes.punchline: Leslie's overly precious attempt at being zen
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's inability to be genuinely calm, even when trying.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I am ready. I am centered. I am one with the universe. Namaste.

jokes.text: We are so [bleep] screwed.
jokes.setup: Leslie just delivered a zen-like response to Ben
jokes.punchline: Her immediate return to panic mode
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural payoff to her previous zen attempt. Great contrast and character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, I'm centered. I'm calm. I'm at peace with the universe.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, what day is it? Oh my God, the city council meeting is tomorrow! I haven't prepared my three binders!

jokes.text: Look, I hate it when my students are upset, so I just went out, driving around. I checked a few neighborhood parks. It took barely six hours.
jokes.setup: Joe explains how he found a student's lost dog
jokes.punchline: The casual mention of spending six hours searching
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that shows Joe's almost excessive niceness, explaining why he's Donna's 'Tammy.'

dialogue.speaker: Joe
dialogue.line: I found the student's lost dog by searching the neighborhood for six hours.

jokes.text: I got so many kids from different women, I just get all of their birthdays out of the way one day a year.
jokes.setup: Someone asks whose birthday party they're at
jokes.punchline: Andy's casual explanation of his efficiency approach to multiple baby mamas
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy that reveals Andy's complicated backstory with matter-of-fact delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh, it's nobody's birthday. I just combine them all into one party to save money. Much more efficient.

jokes.text: That gives us 15 minutes of travel time and 15 minutes for you to take a wet-nap shower in the car.
jokes.setup: April explains their schedule between parties
jokes.punchline: The casual inclusion of car hygiene in their logistics
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing April's practical approach to Andy's chaos.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: We've got a party at the Sweetums factory at 2, then we need to swing by the car wash at 3, then we're catering the Rotary Club dinner at 5.

jokes.text: It's about Larry. He is very sick. He has some kind of disease.
jokes.setup: Andy finally reveals his big secret to April
jokes.punchline: The completely incorrect and vague 'secret' about Larry
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that shows Andy's tendency to misinterpret situations.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: April, I have something I need to tell you. It's about Larry.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: What about him?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I found out that Larry... he's not actually from here. I mean, he is, but he wasn't always. And also, I think he might be involved in something. Something big.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Andy, that makes no sense.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I know! That's how you know it's true!

jokes.text: Yes, that's the reason. Good call, babe.
jokes.setup: April explains Andy's nice behavior toward Larry
jokes.punchline: Andy gratefully accepting the cover story
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment but relatively straightforward execution.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: He's not being nice to Larry. He's just grateful that Larry's finally giving him an excuse to stop pretending to like him.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Thank you, Larry. Thank you so much.

jokes.text: Our first item up for bid is two all-access V.I.P. passes to the unity concert. Let's start the bidding at $40.
jokes.setup: Leslie is trying to run a high-value charity auction
jokes.punchline: Starting with an absurdly low bid for VIP passes
jokes.explanation: Sets up Leslie's forced calmness affecting her auctioneering ability.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, we're starting the bidding for VIP passes to the Harvest Festival at just $5!

dialogue.speaker: Auctioneer
dialogue.line: Do I hear $5? Going once...

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, wait. $5 is insulting. These passes are worth at least $500!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, you can't change the starting bid mid-auction.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Fine. But I'm bidding $500 myself.

jokes.text: We're all just calm and happy people enjoying ourselves together on earth.
jokes.setup: Leslie is trying to stay zen during the auction
jokes.punchline: Her forced hippie-esque philosophy
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's unnatural attempt at being zen.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, I'm just going to breathe. In through my nose, out through my mouth. The auction is just energy, and energy is neither created nor destroyed, it's only transformed. So if I transform my anxiety into positive chi, then...

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, you're hyperventilating.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's just my body's way of saying hello to the universe.

jokes.text: I don't have the gavel, so I'm powerless. I respect the hierarchy of the auction!
jokes.setup: Tom suggests Leslie needs more energy as auctioneer
jokes.punchline: Leslie's bureaucratic excuse for her poor performance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - bureaucratic thinking applied to auction dynamics. Excellent character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I was following proper auction protocol. You need to file a Form 27-B with the Parks Department at least 72 hours before any vocal projection over 85 decibels.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That's not a real form.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It is now. I'm making it real right now.

jokes.text: Leslie is already the queen of stress, so I have to be like her stress shamwow. I wrap myself around her, I soak up all of her stress, and then after the kids are born, someone can squeeze me out into a bucket.
jokes.setup: Ben explains his strategy for keeping Leslie calm
jokes.punchline: The extended shamwow metaphor with disgusting implications
jokes.explanation: Brilliant extended metaphor that's both absurd and oddly touching. Highly original and quotable.

jokes.text: Without having to reset the fence.
jokes.setup: Joe offers Ron a woodworking tip
jokes.punchline: Both men finishing the technical explanation in unison
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Ron and Joe's shared expertise, complicating Ron's suspicions.

dialogue.speaker: Joe
dialogue.line: You see, Ron, the key to a perfect joint is to ensure your wood grain is running in the same direction as your—

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: —chisel blade. Yes, I'm aware.

dialogue.speaker: Both
dialogue.line: It's basic carpentry.

jokes.text: Do you remember the time when you put sleeping pills in my soda? I fell right over in the middle of a town hall meeting.
jokes.setup: Donna reminisces with Joe
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of drugging as a fond memory
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy that explains the toxic dynamic between Donna and Joe.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Remember when we used to slip a little something into the punch at those office parties?

dialogue.speaker: Joe
dialogue.line: Those were the days.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Nobody ever suspected a thing. Good times.

jokes.text: You made a video of it and showed it at my birthday party.
jokes.setup: Donna mentions Joe drugging her
jokes.punchline: The additional humiliation of public screening
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that deepens the dysfunction while maintaining comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Joe drugged me. The additional humiliation of public screening.

jokes.text: Yeah, um, I have got an itch that I have not been able to get to all day, and even against the wall, nothing will do it.
jokes.setup: Joe asks how he can make up for past behavior
jokes.punchline: Donna immediately taking advantage with an intimate request
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy that shows how their dynamic immediately returns to dysfunction.

dialogue.speaker: Joe
dialogue.line: How can I make up for past behavior?

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: You can start by giving me a foot massage.

jokes.text: A very wealthy and elegant-looking woman with a diamond-encrusted eye patch just raised her hand.
jokes.setup: Leslie is trying to create excitement at the auction
jokes.punchline: Her elaborate fantasy about a bidder that doesn't exist
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist escalation showing Leslie's desperate creativity under pressure.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, imagine this: A mysterious billionaire walks in. He's tall, handsome, drives a Tesla. He's never heard of Parks and Recreation, so he has no idea what he's bidding on, but he doesn't care because he's SO rich. He sees the gazebo and he goes, 'I'll pay a million dollars.' Just like that. A million dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And then his even richer brother walks in and says, 'I'll pay TWO million dollars,' and they get into this bidding war, and by the end they've raised fifty million dollars for the Parks Department, and they're so grateful they both want to name their children after me.

jokes.text: The sheik has thrown his hat into the ring.
jokes.setup: Leslie continues inventing fake bidders
jokes.punchline: Adding an even more elaborate fictional character
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the fake bidder concept, though slightly less fresh than the first one.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, so we also have a bid from the Sweetums Corporation... no wait, that's real. Um, we have a bid from... Reggie Philbin's cousin, Derek Philbin. He's very interested in the park.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And then there's also Congressman Jam's investment group, 'Jam Enterprises.' They're prepared to offer... I'm making this up, aren't I?

jokes.text: 3,000, says the gentleman with the crazy hat and a monkey on his shoulder.
jokes.setup: Leslie's fake bidding war continues
jokes.punchline: The increasingly ridiculous description of nonexistent bidders
jokes.explanation: Continues the escalation effectively, though the novelty is wearing off.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We have a bid of $50,000 from a gentleman in the back.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Now $75,000 from a very attractive woman who just walked in.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And we have $100,000 from a man who appears to be made entirely of money.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: $125,000 from a mysterious billionaire who communicates only through interpretive dance.

jokes.text: Well, earlier today, I was licking icing off of my finger, and then, boom, I swallow my wedding ring.
jokes.setup: Larry explains his 'embarrassing' situation to April
jokes.punchline: The completely mundane reality versus Andy's dramatic interpretation
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection payoff that shows how Andy misinterpreted an innocent situation.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: April, I need to tell you something embarrassing that happened to me.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Okay?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I was at the grocery store and I tripped in the produce section and fell into a display of watermelons.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: That's it?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Dude, Larry didn't just fall — he LAUNCHED himself through the air like a heat-seeking missile and demolished an entire pyramid of watermelons. People were screaming. A child cried. Security had to be called.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I tripped and knocked over maybe three watermelons.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It was the most catastrophic produce incident since the great cantaloupe shortage of '09.

jokes.text: It's just a matter of getting out the old colander.
jokes.setup: Larry explains his ring-swallowing problem
jokes.punchline: The disturbing implication about his retrieval method
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy in suggesting something gross without saying it explicitly. Good dark comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: So I swallowed my wedding ring. The doctor said I'd have to wait for nature to take its course. My wife asked how I was going to get it back, and I said, 'Well, I guess we'll just have to see what comes out.' She didn't think that was funny.

jokes.text: Just think about all their great songs, covering everything from the culture of Southern California to drug use in Southern California.
jokes.setup: Ben is trying to sell the Red Hot Chili Peppers guitar
jokes.punchline: His accurate but reductive summary of their entire catalog
jokes.explanation: Brilliant observational comedy with perfect structure and economy. Sharp cultural commentary delivered deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: The Red Hot Chili Peppers have written approximately 200 songs about California, socks, and the lyrics 'funky monkey' repeated over and over.

jokes.text: Why would you erase the signatures?
jokes.setup: Someone erased the Red Hot Chili Peppers signatures
jokes.punchline: Ben's incredulous reaction to the destruction of value
jokes.explanation: Standard reaction beat, though it fits Ben's character.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Wait, someone erased the Red Hot Chili Peppers signatures? Do you know how much that's worth? That's a collectible! That's like... that's like taking the Mona Lisa and drawing a mustache on it!

jokes.text: I'm more of a Jack Johnson guy.
jokes.setup: Herman erased the Red Hot Chili Peppers signatures
jokes.punchline: His casual music preference as justification for destroying value
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist logic and economy, though the setup is simple.

dialogue.speaker: Herman
dialogue.line: Look, I like what I like. The Red Hot Chili Peppers are okay, but they're not really my thing. I'm more of a Nickelback guy.

jokes.text: It's not even the original lineup.
jokes.setup: Herman continues justifying erasing the signatures
jokes.punchline: His music snob reasoning for destroying valuable memorabilia
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Herman's music snobbery, though predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Herman
dialogue.line: Look, those signatures were in ballpoint pen. Ballpoint! Do you know what kind of ink degradation we're talking about here? In fifty years, those signatures would be completely illegible. I was doing a public service.

dialogue.speaker: Herman
dialogue.line: Plus, the signatures were crowding the album cover. The negative space was completely unbalanced. It's called composition, people.

jokes.text: You have very weak hands.
jokes.setup: Ben gets injured trying to get the guitar back
jokes.punchline: Herman's insulting medical assessment
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy moment with nice economy.

dialogue.speaker: Herman
dialogue.line: Well, the good news is you're going to live. The bad news is you're still you.

jokes.text: You know the EMT said that if flea had sent you his bass, you could've lost a thumb.
jokes.setup: Leslie comforting Ben after his injury
jokes.punchline: The specific and random nature of the alternative injury scenario
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist specificity, though the structure is loose.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're going to be fine. At least you didn't get hit by a blimp.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: A blimp?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yeah, a blimp could've hit you. Then you'd really be in trouble.

jokes.text: When we're together, all we do is have sex, sit on the couch, eat homemade pasta, have sex again.
jokes.setup: Donna explains why Joe is her Tammy
jokes.punchline: Listing objectively wonderful things as complaints
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic comedy that perfectly captures why healthy relationships feel boring to some people.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Joe is my Tammy because he's successful, he's handsome, he's well-dressed, he makes a lot of money, and he's extremely organized.

jokes.text: It's a nightmare. I become boring.
jokes.setup: Donna describes her relationship with Joe
jokes.punchline: Her horror at becoming a normal, happy person
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment with great economy showing Donna's self-awareness about her drama addiction.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Joe makes me happy. We have a great relationship. He's kind, he's thoughtful, he listens to me.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: And honestly, it scares me to death.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I'm becoming a normal person with normal problems and a normal relationship. What is happening to me?

jokes.text: Don't confuse drama with happiness.
jokes.setup: Donna explains her dysfunctional relationship pattern
jokes.punchline: Ron's simple wisdom delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and wisdom. Perfect Ron moment that's both funny and insightful.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You just described every relationship I've ever had.

jokes.text: They're moving again to China...town in France.
jokes.setup: Andy is desperately making up secrets
jokes.punchline: His ridiculous geographic confusion
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Andy's geographic confusion under pressure.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I have a secret. I think... I think Austria and Australia are the same place.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: They're not.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Really? Because I've been saying it wrong my whole life.

jokes.text: I noticed that your right rear tire was a little flat, so I patched it, and I inflated it for you.
jokes.setup: Joe continues being perfectly nice
jokes.punchline: His unsolicited car maintenance
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Joe's helpful nature, though straightforward execution.

dialogue.speaker: Joe
dialogue.line: I noticed your car's oil was looking a little dark, so I went ahead and changed it for you. Also rotated your tires, replaced your air filter, and detailed the interior. No need to thank me!

jokes.text: I'll wear that red thing when you deserve to see me in that red thing.
jokes.setup: Joe requests Donna wear something specific
jokes.punchline: Donna's power play response
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Donna regaining control, though fairly standard.

jokes.text: I mean, hey, you're looking at a woman who just hit triple cherries in her uterus.
jokes.setup: Leslie suggests they might win the lottery
jokes.punchline: Comparing triplets to a slot machine jackpot
jokes.explanation: Brilliant metaphor that's both funny and oddly touching. Great originality and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: If we win the lottery, we could have triplets!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, the odds of winning the lottery are like one in 175 million.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So you're saying there's a chance? Because having triplets is basically hitting the jackpot. Cha-ching!

jokes.text: Half of my tuition was paid for by the Indiana scholarship for pretty blondes who like to read. It's now called the Virginia Woolf prize--different time.
jokes.setup: Leslie suggests their kids will get scholarships
jokes.punchline: Her casual mention of a hilariously outdated scholarship criteria
jokes.explanation: Excellent social commentary disguised as character backstory. Great structure and very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Our kids are going to get scholarships. I'm talking about academic scholarships, athletic scholarships, and scholarships for having really good penmanship.

jokes.text: We spent too much money on macaroons.
jokes.setup: Leslie panics about their Paris spending
jokes.punchline: The specific and frivolous nature of their regretted purchase
jokes.explanation: Good specificity that makes the financial panic feel real and relatable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, we spent HOW much on that baguette warmer?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: It was in the shape of the Eiffel Tower, Leslie.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That doesn't make it a necessary purchase!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: You said, and I quote, 'Every baguette deserves to be warm and patriotic.'

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I was in PARIS, Ben. I was emotionally compromised!

jokes.text: I am immune to stress because I have you.
jokes.setup: Leslie explains why she's suddenly calm
jokes.punchline: Her sweet and logical explanation
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment that works both comedically and emotionally.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I realized that worrying doesn't help anything. I'm just going to focus on the things I can control and let go of the things I can't. It's actually very freeing.

jokes.text: Ron is King Sparkle of Cupcake Forest.
jokes.setup: Ron has been playing with the children
jokes.punchline: His ridiculous fairy tale title
jokes.explanation: Perfect contrast with Ron's usual persona. Excellent character development through humor.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: Once upon a time, there was a brave knight named Sir Ronald of Swanson, who slayed dragons with his bare hands and built his own castle out of wood he personally harvested.

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: That's not a real fairy tale!

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: It's called 'The Woodworker and the Eggplant Parmesan.' It's a classic.

jokes.text: You're King Sparkle of Cupcake Forest. It suits you.
jokes.setup: Donna observes Ron's character growth
jokes.punchline: Claiming the ridiculous title actually fits him
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that reinforces character growth with humor.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: You know what? I think Ron has actually grown as a person. He's become more open, more collaborative, more human.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Thank you, Donna. That means a lot.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Yeah. You're basically a 'Sensitive Government Warrior' now.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm going to pretend you didn't say that.

jokes.text: Yes, I did it! I kept a secret.
jokes.setup: Andy finally reveals the pregnancy secret
jokes.punchline: His pride in accomplishing the basic task
jokes.explanation: Good character payoff showing Andy's pride in basic competence.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: We're pregnant! April and I are gonna have a baby!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I did it. I accomplished something. Me. Andy Dwyer.

jokes.text: I'm sure you will both raise a wonderful child with whom I will profoundly disagree on nearly everything.
jokes.setup: Ron congratulates Leslie and Ben
jokes.punchline: His honest prediction about disagreeing with their child
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron moment - sincere congratulations combined with honest political differences. Excellent character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Congratulations on your child. I'm sure it will be a fine human being. But mark my words, Leslie, Ben... that child will disagree with you. And when it does, I will be there... to agree with the child.

jokes.text: First my cousin Winona gets into a car accident the night my one-man show opens, and now this? Why are my accomplishments always overshadowed?
jokes.setup: Everyone celebrates Leslie and Ben's pregnancy news
jokes.punchline: Craig making it about himself in the most absurd way possible
jokes.explanation: Perfect Craig moment showing his narcissistic worldview. Good structure and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: This is great news for Leslie and Ben, but let's talk about what this means for me. I'm going to be the best godfather ever. Actually, I'm going to be better than a godfather. I'm going to be a god-uncle. That's a thing I just made up and it's trademarked.

tmdb_overview: Leslie, Ben, and Andy head to San Francisco for a National Parks Conference. Tom opens his restaurant, Ron has another run-in with his ex-wife Tammy 2, Andy lives a rock and roll fantasy, and Leslie makes a huge decision about her future.
insight: Jerry's incompetence reaches critical mass: 2.51 jokes per minute sustain the chaos.
